findings.issue: The R16 'initOAuth call sites pass AbortSignal' claim is false on the current branch. `initOAuth` and `oauthApi.init` accept an `AbortSignal` parameter (remote-frontend/src/api.ts:33-34, remote-frontend/src/lib/api/oauth.ts:21-22), but neither call site passes one: `AppRouter.tsx:50` calls `initOAuth(provider, callbackUrl, challenge)` with only three arguments, and `InvitationPage.tsx:52` calls `initOAuth(provider, returnTo, challenge)` with only three. The `signal` parameter is therefore dead from the call-site perspective, and the R16 plumbing is incomplete. If a user navigates away from `/login` or `/invitations/:token/accept` while the POST /v1/oauth/web/init is in flight, the request cannot be cancelled, the in-flight `fetch` keeps running, and any state updates from the eventual response fire on an unmounted component. This is exactly the StrictMode double-init hazard R16 was meant to close for the redeem path (see `oauthApi.redeem` at AppRouter.tsx:142 and InvitationCompletePage.tsx:56-61 which correctly thread the signal). The R16 gate was passed because no test asserts the fourth argument â€” both `AppRouter.test.tsx:108-112` and `InvitationPage.test.tsx:67-71` only `toHaveBeenCalledWith(provider, returnTo, challenge)`, not `expect.any(AbortSignal)`. A regression removing the signal parameter entirely would still pass all current tests.
findings.remediation: Mirror the redeem pattern: in both `handleOAuthLogin` bodies, create `const controller = new AbortController()` at the top of the try block, pass `controller.signal` as the fourth argument to `initOAuth`, and abort it in a `useEffect` cleanup or in a wrapper. Then update the two assertions above to `expect.stringMatching(/^Bearer$|^[A-Za-z0-9_+/=.-]+$/)` ... `expect.any(AbortSignal)` (or, more simply, assert the 4-arg form `toHaveBeenCalledWith('github', returnToContaining, challenge64, expect.any(AbortSignal))`) so a regression that drops the signal is caught.

findings.issue: Carry-forward of R19 minimax-F4: `InvitationPage.tsx:50` wraps `token` in `encodeURIComponent` when building the OAuth returnTo URL, but the only test exercising this code path is `starts invitation OAuth with fallback PKCE challenge when crypto.subtle is unavailable` (InvitationPage.test.tsx:46-77), which uses the URL-safe token `'invite-token'` (line 26 initialEntries, line 69 assertion contains `/invitations/invite-token/complete`). For this token `encodeURIComponent` is a no-op, so a regression that drops the encoding (returning to the R17 behavior where the token is interpolated raw) would not be caught. The decision-ledger's R15 entry claims 'encodeURIComponent for tokens' was applied, but no test was added at that time to lock the behavior in. The test for the `getInvitation` URL at `api.ts:43` similarly only exercises `encodeURIComponent` against the same URL-safe token.
findings.remediation: Add a test that renders `/invitations/invite%2Btoken/accept` (token 'invite+token') or `/invitations/invite%2Ftoken/accept` (token 'invite/token'), clicks Continue with GitHub, and asserts `initOAuth` was called with a returnTo containing the percent-encoded form (e.g. `expect.stringContaining('/invitations/invite%2Btoken/complete')`). Use a fresh describe block to avoid interfering with the URL-safe token test.

findings.issue: `oauth.test.ts` covers the success and `init` error paths but has no direct unit test for `oauthApi.redeem`'s error path. The `init` error path is covered at oauth.test.ts:160-172 (asserts `init` throws when `response.ok` is false). The `redeem` analogue is missing â€” the only redeem failure coverage is the indirect component-level test at `AppRouter.test.tsx:312-331`, which asserts the redirect query string but not the `ApiError` shape (status, response). If a future refactor changes `oauthApi.redeem` to throw a plain `Error` instead of `ApiError`, the component-level test would still pass (it only checks the error message string), and any code that depends on `err.status` (e.g. a future retry-on-401 path) would silently regress.
findings.remediation: Add a test that mocks `fetch` to resolve with `{ ok: false, status: 400, json: async () => ({...}) }` for the redeem endpoint and asserts `oauthApi.redeem('h', 'a', 'v')` rejects with an `ApiError` whose `status` is 400 and whose `message` matches `/oauth redeem failed: 400/`.

findings.issue: `OAuthCallbackPage` (AppRouter.tsx:105-182) cleans up the `oauth_verifier` sessionStorage key in every error branch (lines 120-121, 127-128, 135-137, 155-156) and in the success branch (lines 147-148), but it never cleans up `localStorage` entries that may be left behind by a previous successful login. The flow stores `access_token` in localStorage on success (line 146). If a user logs in successfully, then signs out via a path that does not clear `access_token` (the `oauthApi.logout` flow at oauth.ts:70-86 clears it, but other paths â€” e.g. manual clearing of sessionStorage via DevTools, or a `ProfileProvider` 401 removal at ProfileProvider.tsx:59 â€” may leave a stale token), and then revisits `/oauth/callback?error=access_denied`, the error path runs but does NOT clear the stale `access_token`. A subsequent navigation to a guarded route (e.g. `/nodes`) would attempt to use the stale token, hitting a 401 and getting bounced back to login. The `InvitationCompletePage` does the same dance (clears sessionStorage but does not clear localStorage on the error branches at lines 29-30, 36-37, 45, 52, 82-83) â€” but the known-gap list explicitly documents `makeRequest` auto-attaching the stale Bearer token to OAuth calls, which makes the stale-token-on-error-callback path a concrete cross-feature interaction, not just a theoretical one.
findings.remediation: On every error branch in `OAuthCallbackPage.completeOAuth` (the four `window.location.assign('/login?error=...')` exits and the catch block) and in `InvitationCompletePage.completeInvitation` (the `oauthError`, missing-params, missing-verifier, missing-token, and catch branches), also call `localStorage.removeItem('access_token')` alongside `clearVerifier()` / `clearInvitationToken()`. Then add an assertion to the existing error-path tests in `AppRouter.test.tsx` (e.g. 'oauth callback: handles oauthError param' at line 255) that pre-populates `localStorage.setItem('access_token', 'stale')` and asserts it is cleared after the error redirect.

findings.issue: `pkce.ts:3` `generateVerifier` calls `globalThis.crypto.getRandomValues(array)` without optional chaining, so a stub that replaces `globalThis.crypto` with `undefined` (or with an object that lacks `getRandomValues`) would throw `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'getRandomValues')`. The sibling function `sha256` at lines 14-24 uses `globalThis.crypto?.subtle` and a `typeof subtle?.digest === 'function'` check, so it gracefully falls back. The two functions are therefore asymmetric in their degradation: `generateChallenge` can survive a hostile `crypto` shim, but `generateVerifier` cannot. In practice this never fires (every browser that supports `crypto.subtle` also supports `crypto.getRandomValues`), but the `pkce.test.ts` suite does not exercise the 'no getRandomValues' case, so a future test that stubs `crypto` to `undefined` (e.g. for an SSR-style 'crypto not available' simulation) would crash rather than skip. The fix would be a one-liner with no behavior change in any real environment.
findings.remediation: Make `generateVerifier` symmetric with `sha256`: add a feature check, and if `getRandomValues` is unavailable, throw a typed `Error` (e.g. `new Error('crypto.getRandomValues is required for PKCE')`) rather than letting the TypeError bubble. Optionally add a pkce.test.ts case that stubs `crypto` to `{}` and asserts `generateVerifier` throws the typed error instead of a generic `TypeError`.
